hero-title: Burnout and Energy: How to Stay Well While Using AI at Work
Burnout and Resilience: Looking After Your Energy in the Age of AI
Burnout and Resilience: Managing Human Energy in the AI Age
Burnout and Resilience: Managing Human Energy Under AI
Burnout and Resilience in the AI Age
lead: Burnout is not a strange or random thing. It happens for clear reasons, and those reasons grow stronger when you are under pressure. The good news is that once you can see those reasons, you can do something about each one. You can act early, before you get too tired or worn down.
Burnout is not a mystery. It comes from a few specific things that build up under pressure. Once you understand what they are, you have clear levers you can pull early — before the harm sets in.
Burnout is not a mystery. It is a predictable outcome of specific variables interacting under pressure. Understanding those variables gives you levers to pull before the damage is done.
Burnout is predictable: specific variables interacting under pressure. Understand them and you gain levers to pull before damage sets in.
Burnout is a predictable product of specific variables under pressure. Name them; pull the levers early.
enduring: A question to sit with as you read: Burnout is not your fault and it does not mean you are weak — that part you can hold onto. But here is the question the rest of this page asks you to test: does burnout really happen for reasons you can name — doubting yourself, not knowing what is wanted, missing information, and not enough time? And if most of those reasons come from the situation around you rather than from inside you, does it follow that the best way to build strength is to fix the situation, not just try harder as a person? See whether your own experience agrees, and feel free to push back.

A question worth sitting with: Burnout is not a personal failing — but is it the predictable result of self-doubt, expectation gaps, missing information, and time pressure? If most of those forces come from the environment around you rather than from within, does that make resilience something you build into the system, not only into the person? Weigh it against what you have lived, and disagree if it does not fit.

A question worth sitting with: Burnout is not a personal failing — but is it the predictable result of self-doubt, expectation gaps, information asymmetry, and time pressure? If most of those forces turn out to be environmental rather than personal, does that make resilience something you build into the system, not just into the person? Test it against your own experience as you read, and keep the freedom to disagree.

A question to weigh: Burnout is no personal failing — but is it the predictable result of self-doubt, expectation gaps, information asymmetry, and time pressure? If most are environmental, is resilience built into the system, not just the person? Decide for yourself.
Structural, or personal? Burnout from self-doubt, expectation gaps, information asymmetry, time pressure — if these are environmental, build resilience into the system? You decide.
worked-title: A real example: how Ravi turned an overwhelming quarter into one she could handle
Worked example: Ravi's quarter, from overload to manageable
Worked example: Ravi's quarter from overload to navigable

worked-setup: Ravi is a product engineer. She has just been given a big, messy project that connects several systems together. Nobody has told her clearly what it should do, and it is due at the end of the quarter. By the second week she feels worried. She is not sure what "finished" should even look like. She keeps comparing her rough early work to the polished AI-made mockups her manager drops into the chat, and they look much better than hers. Quietly, she has decided the whole project will only work if she is brilliant. So she doubts herself a lot, the gap between what she thinks she must do and what she feels able to do feels huge, and she has not told anyone how she feels — meanwhile the deadline keeps creeping closer.

Ravi is a product engineer who has just been handed a big, messy integration project with a vague brief and an end-of-quarter deadline. By week two she is anxious: she does not really know what "done" looks like, she keeps comparing her rough drafts to the polished AI-generated mockups her manager pastes into chat, and she has quietly assumed the whole thing depends on her being brilliant. Her self-doubt is high, the expectation gap feels enormous, and she has told no one — so the timeline is silently running down.

Ravi is a product engineer who has just been handed a sprawling integration project with a fuzzy brief and an end-of-quarter deadline. By week two she is anxious: she does not really know what "done" looks like, she keeps comparing her rough drafts to the slick AI-generated mockups her manager pastes into chat, and she has quietly assumed the whole thing rests on her being brilliant. Her self-doubt is high, the expectation gap feels enormous, and she has told no one — so the timeline is silently burning down.

Ravi, a product engineer, inherits a sprawling integration project: fuzzy brief, end-of-quarter deadline. By week two she is anxious — no clear definition of "done," her rough drafts measured against slick AI mockups her manager pastes into chat, and a private assumption that it all rests on her brilliance. High self-doubt, an enormous expectation gap, no one told — the timeline silently burning down.

equation-intro: We can think of burnout as something built from four parts. This is not just a nice picture or a comparison. It really works like a sum: it points to the exact things that push people toward feeling exhausted and switched-off at work.
We can model burnout as a function of four variables. This is not just a metaphor — it works as an equation, naming the specific forces that push people toward exhaustion and disengagement:

trust-intro: The burnout equation shows the forces that push someone toward breaking down. But burnout does not happen all on its own. It happens while we work alongside other people, and trust works against burnout, pushing back the other way. The balanced version of the equation shows this clearly:

The burnout equation shows the forces pushing toward breakdown. But burnout does not happen in isolation — it happens within a shared, collaborative setting where trust acts as a counterforce. The balanced equation makes this clear:

The burnout equation describes the forces pushing toward breakdown. But burnout does not happen in isolation — it happens within a collaborative context where trust acts as a counterforce. The balanced equation makes this explicit:

The burnout equation describes the forces pushing toward breakdown — but burnout never happens in isolation. It occurs within a collaborative context where trust acts as a counterforce. The balanced equation makes this explicit:
Burnout occurs in a collaborative context where trust counters it. The balanced equation makes this explicit:
low-trust: When trust is low, everything flips the other way. If you are not sure your work will be judged fairly, you doubt yourself more. If feedback is unclear or keeps changing, you understand less about what is wanted. Expectations feel pushed onto you rather than agreed together. Every part at the top of the sum grows, while the time you have shrinks. That is why workplaces with low trust wear people out so fast — the maths is working against them.

When trust is low, the opposite happens. Not knowing whether your work will be judged fairly inflates self-doubt. Unclear or inconsistent feedback widens the information gap. Expectations feel imposed rather than agreed. Every variable on top of the equation grows while the time available shrinks. That is why low-trust workplaces are burnout factories — the maths is stacked against resilience.

When trust is low, the opposite happens. Uncertainty about whether your work will be judged fairly inflates self-doubt. Unclear or inconsistent feedback widens the information gap. Expectations feel imposed rather than negotiated. Every variable in the numerator grows while the denominator shrinks. This is why low-trust environments are burnout factories — the mathematics are stacked against resilience.

When trust is low, the opposite holds. Uncertainty over fair judgment inflates self-doubt; inconsistent feedback widens the information gap; expectations feel imposed, not negotiated. Every numerator variable grows while the denominator shrinks. Low-trust environments are burnout factories — the mathematics stacked against resilience.
Low trust reverses everything: self-doubt inflates, the information gap widens, expectations feel imposed. The numerator grows, the denominator shrinks. Low-trust environments are burnout factories.
ai-pressure: New AI tools have added a fresh kind of pressure, and it lands right on the expectation gap. When an AI can write a first draft in a few seconds, it starts to feel like everyone should be that fast. When AI work looks neat and finished, it starts to feel like everyone's work should look that polished. So now the person is being measured — sometimes openly, sometimes quietly — against a machine. And that machine never gets tired, never needs a break, and never doubts itself.

AI tools have added a specific new pressure on the expectation gap. When an AI can generate a first draft in seconds, the perceived standard for speed and volume rises. When AI output looks polished, the perceived standard for quality rises. The human worker is now being benchmarked — openly or quietly — against a machine that does not get tired, does not need breaks, and never feels self-doubt.

The rise of AI tools has introduced a specific new pressure on the expectation gap. When an AI can generate a first draft in seconds, the perceived standard for speed and volume rises. When AI output looks polished on the surface, the perceived standard for quality rises. The human worker is now being benchmarked — explicitly or implicitly — against a machine that does not get tired, does not need breaks, and does not experience self-doubt.

AI tools impose a specific new pressure on the expectation gap. When an AI drafts in seconds, the perceived standard for speed and volume rises; when its output looks polished, the standard for quality rises. The worker is now benchmarked — explicitly or implicitly — against a machine that never tires, never needs breaks, and never doubts itself.
AI pressures the expectation gap: instant drafts raise the speed standard, polished output raises the quality standard. Workers are benchmarked against a machine that never tires, rests, or doubts.
ai-reframe: The augmented intelligence approach looks at the whole thing differently. The AI is not the thing you are trying to beat. It is more like a support, a frame to build on. The real question is not "can you work as fast as the AI?" The real question is "can you use the AI to make something better than either you or the AI could make alone?" Asking it this way closes the gap, because it changes what is being expected of you in the first place.

The augmented intelligence approach reframes the relationship. AI is not the benchmark — it is the scaffolding. The question is not "can you produce output as fast as the AI?" It is "can you use the AI to produce better output than either of you could alone?" This reframing closes the expectation gap by changing what the expectation is.

The augmented intelligence approach reframes the relationship. AI is not the benchmark — it is the scaffolding. The question is not "can you produce output as fast as the AI?" It is "can you use the AI to produce better output than either of you could produce alone?" This reframing closes the expectation gap by changing what the expectation is.

The augmented intelligence approach reframes it: AI is not the benchmark but the scaffolding. The question is not "can you match the AI's speed?" but "can you use the AI to produce better output than either could alone?" This closes the expectation gap by changing the expectation itself.
The augmented intelligence approach reframes AI as scaffolding, not benchmark: not "match its speed" but "use it for output neither could reach alone." Changing the expectation closes the gap.
trap-title: The trap: "AI will do more of my work, so I'll feel less stressed"
The trap: "AI will reduce my workload, so I'll be less stressed"
The trap: "AI will reduce my workload, so I'll be less stressed"
The trap: "AI cuts my workload, so I'll be less stressed"
The trap: "AI cuts workload, so less stress"
trap-body: It sounds obvious — give the boring work to the machine and you can breathe more easily. But stress does not really come from how many hours you work. It comes from the gap between what people expect and what you feel you can actually deliver. When AI makes a task faster, the expectation just grows to fill the gap: more features, more polish, more work in the same amount of time. So the gap does not close — it just moves somewhere else. Unless you stop and clearly agree on what "enough" means, the new normal swallows up every bit of time you saved, and the pressure stays exactly where it was. Sometimes it gets worse, because now the work is "supposed" to be easy.

It feels obvious — hand the boring work to the machine and breathe easier. But stress is not driven by raw hours; it is driven by the gap between what is expected and what you believe you can deliver. When AI makes a task faster, the expectation simply rises to match: more features, more polish, more output in the same window. The gap does not close, it relocates. Unless you deliberately renegotiate what "enough" means, the new baseline absorbs every efficiency gain and the pressure stays exactly where it was — sometimes higher, because now the work "should" be easy.

It feels obvious — hand the grind to the machine and breathe easier. But stress is not driven by raw hours; it is driven by the gap between what is expected and what you believe you can deliver. When AI makes a task faster, the expectation simply rises to match: more features, more polish, more output in the same window. The expectation gap does not close, it relocates. Unless you deliberately renegotiate what "enough" means, the new baseline absorbs every efficiency gain and the pressure stays exactly where it was — sometimes higher, because now the work "should" be easy.

It feels obvious — hand the grind to the machine and breathe easier. But stress is not driven by raw hours; it tracks the gap between what is expected and what you believe you can deliver. When AI speeds a task, the expectation rises to match: more features, more polish, more output in the same window. The gap does not close, it relocates. Unless you renegotiate what "enough" means, the new baseline absorbs every efficiency gain and pressure holds — sometimes higher, because now the work "should" be easy.

Obvious, but wrong: stress tracks the expectation gap, not hours. Speeding a task just raises expectations to match — the gap relocates, not closes. Without renegotiating "enough," the new baseline absorbs every gain and pressure holds, sometimes higher.
structural-intro: The usual advice about burnout puts all the weight on you as a person: look after yourself, set boundaries, manage your energy. These are not bad ideas, but on their own they are not enough. The burnout equation shows that most of the things causing burnout come from around you, not from inside you. Self-doubt is made worse by the culture you work in. The expectation gap is shaped by the people leading you. Missing information is a sign that communication has broken down. And how much time you have is decided by how the work was planned.

The usual approach to burnout puts the burden on the individual: practise self-care, set boundaries, manage your energy. These are not bad suggestions, but they are incomplete. The burnout equation shows that most of the variables are environmental, not personal. Self-doubt is amplified by culture. The expectation gap is shaped by leadership. Information asymmetry is a communication failure. Time buffers are a planning decision.

The conventional approach to burnout puts the burden on the individual: practise self-care, set boundaries, manage your energy. These are not bad suggestions, but they are incomplete. The burnout equation shows that most of the variables are environmental, not personal. Self-doubt is amplified by culture. The expectation gap is shaped by leadership. Information asymmetry is a communication failure. Time buffers are a planning decision.

The conventional approach to burnout burdens the individual: self-care, boundaries, energy management. Not bad, but incomplete. The burnout equation shows most variables are environmental, not personal. Culture amplifies self-doubt; leadership shapes the expectation gap; information asymmetry is a communication failure; time buffers are a planning decision.

Conventional burnout advice burdens the individual — self-care, boundaries. Incomplete: most variables are environmental. Culture amplifies self-doubt, leadership shapes the gap, asymmetry is a communication failure, time buffers a planning decision.
structural-resilience: So resilience is mostly something built into teams and systems, rather than a special quality some people are born with. A team that has clear goals, gives feedback early, talks openly, and has high trust will hold up well, even if some people on it sometimes doubt themselves. A team without those things will burn out, even if every single person on it is hard-working and disciplined.

So resilience is mainly a structural property of teams and systems rather than an individual character trait. A team with clear criteria, early feedback loops, open communication, and high trust will be resilient even if individual members sometimes feel self-doubt. A team without those structures will burn out even if every member is individually disciplined.

Resilience, therefore, is primarily a structural property of teams and systems rather than an individual character trait. A team with clear criteria, early feedback loops, transparent communication, and high trust will be resilient even if individual members experience occasional self-doubt. A team without those structures will burn out even if every member is individually disciplined.

Resilience is therefore primarily structural — a property of teams and systems, not an individual trait. A team with clear criteria, early feedback loops, transparent communication, and high trust stays resilient despite occasional individual self-doubt. One without those structures burns out even when every member is disciplined.

Resilience is structural — a property of teams, not traits. Clear criteria, early feedback, transparent communication, and high trust keep a team resilient despite individual self-doubt; their absence burns out even disciplined members.
tryit-intro: Think of the project that is worrying you the most right now. This week, pull just one resilience lever: the milestone-review lever. Setting up an early review buys you back some time (the time buffer, τ) and helps you spot, early on, any gaps between what is expected and what you can do.
Pick the project weighing on you most right now and pull a single resilience lever this week — the milestone-review lever, which buys back time buffer (τ) and surfaces hidden expectation gaps early.
